research_answer: Even when restricting the analysis to developed countries — where colonial-era trade distortions, resource extraction policies, and structural constraints are less relevant — forward linkages still fail to meaningfully predict export transitions. While the joint-exporting coefficient for developed countries is slightly higher (0.77 vs. the broader sample), the transition coefficient — which measures whether countries actually move into downstream sectors they didn't previously export — is only 0.016 and is not economically significant. This suggests the weakness of forward linkages as a driver of structural transformation is not a phenomenon unique to developing economies with institutional barriers; it appears to be a general feature of how economies diversify.
supporting_quotes: Even when limiting attention to developed countries with no colonial legacy, patterns of production are not associated with forward linkages in a significant way. Although the probability of joint exporting along vertical linkage lines is slightly higher among developed countries, it is not economically significant.

data_points.metric: Forward linkage coefficient - joint exporting, developed countries
Forward linkage coefficient - transition measure, developed countries
